result:
I would give every human the reflexive habit of asking themselves—before any action or belief—“What happens next, to everyone?”

It isn’t a rule imposed by law or decree; it would simply be part of breathing, like noticing when you’re cold. You lift an object, pollute a river, post a rumor, cast a vote, open a door for someone, or fail to open it: the same instant, an internal mirror flicks on, showing the cascade of consequences stretching far beyond you and far into the future. You see the child who drinks that river, the stranger who reads the rumor, the billions who inherit the climate you nudge. You feel the weight, not as guilt alone, but as proportionate responsibility.

This habit would not melt all conflict, erase selfishness, or conjure perfect decisions into being, but it would constantly enlarge the frame of reference. Cruelties that now require deliberate forgetting would lose their foothold. Inventions that aid everyone would rise faster because the payoff would naturally include the widest possible net of beneficiaries. Apathy would begin to ache the way an unfelt injury aches when blood returns to it.

In short, the world would remain complicated, yet every second of choice would carry an expanded sense of who and what is at stake—until ill will and neglect require the same strenuous effort that empathy now does.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
